The problem is that there is no guaranteed order of replies (even if that's what it looks like most of the time) and I don't want that to be an implicit assumption for the code to work properly.  The sends are guaranteed to go out in the order written but the remote end is free to reply in any order.
So in my push I did it so that we wait until verification from prior replies and then send the request.

For every repository I've checked (except for the now-defunct "servo" repo[1]), `attrs.author` is *only* the plain email address. In the servo repo, it's in the form "name \<email\>".

Thus, after the split, only the old, defunct servo repository has two pieces. Everything else only has the one for the email address. If you look at pushes on servo, authorEmail is pretty much always going to be "bors-servo", but everywhere else, it would be the actual author email.

So this PR in total changes:
* the tooltip from showing the email address to just a generic "this user"
* the push titlebar from showing author name (in most cases, this is actually the email address) to always showing the email address

1. https://treeherder.mozilla.org/#/jobs?repo=servo

I explained the signal transferring portion in the comment.

As to the `hasattr` check that is because of a whole other issue. The issue boils down to we need one shared event handler with no shared state that can indicate to a stream that the files is being read for the specific reason of being transferred to S3. We need one shared handler because one handler per request really bogs down the system. We need no share state because the handler could be invoked by separate threads.

So to achieve this, the current strategy is to check for a method on the stream and call it in the handlers to enable/disable transfer reading functionality. This is what we had in place for callbacks. However if the stream is wrapped multiple times and two of these wrappers require this enabling/disabling functionality, before only the top level wrapper would have its method called and the wrapper underneath would not have its method called. So instead the top level wrapper needs to be able to signal to the wrapper underneath that it was signaled to enable/disable whatever functionality it needs to enable/disable and so it will need to as well. So I tried to generalize all of this with the `signal_transferring()` and `signal_not_transferring()` methods. I'm up for suggestions on how to improve this.

If you would like to have the configuration plaintext, you can always:
```
{  ..., 'scope_manager': 'opentracing.TornadoScopeManager' }
```

This is a common approach in Django, where you import the module to avoid people importing it in their code, mostly to avoid the side effect of the import at configuration time. In our case it doesn't change so much, so it's more a matter of style. I would say to avoid `tornado` because it means keeping an internal mapping (and I prefer to be explicit). Probably you can just pass the string of the class that we import and initialize in the Tracer constructor.

I didn't design it this way so much as just evolve it; initially I thought all I needed was a few import shims, then as it became clear I needed alternate implementations of some queries, I just stuck them into the same format. I also thought this could maybe use some cleanup, just ran out of time; that's partly why the PR title says WIP still.

In terms of what we should do, there are a couple orthogonal issues:

1) Define a proper interface and then inherit two versions of it. This seems like probably a clearer way to structure it, regardless of how we decide which version to use.

2) Version-checking vs catching ImportError. In the past I've leaned towards the latter, because it seems like a more direct check of the thing you really care about (and in some cases plays better with someone using an intermediate/development version for whatever reason). But in this case the distinguishing import is pretty subtle (`_GenericAlias`), and I agree it would probably be clearer to version-check.

Note that the last profile in the chain is the profile that you originally wanted to install (by selecting it in portal_setup / Import tab) and the earlier ones in the chain are its dependencies. I have a personal style preference that if a function needs to own some data, it should take an owned version of it, rather than taking a reference and cloning it, because that's more upfront about the cost. So personally I would change this function signature to take a `PathBuf` instead of a `&Path`, rather than doing the cloning here. In this case, it avoids the clone entirely, because the caller is actually doing: `work_dir_buf.to_os_string().as_ref()`, so rather than doing `work_dir_buf.to_os_string().as_ref().clone().to_path_buf()` we should end up just doing `PathBuf::from(work_dir_buf.to_os_string())` and being happy.

(Also, I think `work_dir.clone().to_path_buf()` could be re-written as just `work_dir.to_path_buf()`, but the above is probably better :))

Because it's not really feasible to swap out different trainers.  Taking these dependencies as inputs instead of instantiating them directly in the model is only useful when there are multiple valid alternatives and we want to experiment with them.  That's not true of our `DecoderTrainers`, and it's not clear to me that it will ever be true.  Also, because `DecoderTrainer` is generic over the supervision type, it only really makes sense to allow `DecoderTrainers` that match the supervision type that your model has, and we can't enforce that with our dependency injection.

If you find yourself wanting to try a few different training options, you'll first have to make sure that your model has the right supervision available for all of them.  Then you can add some flags to switch between them, but I'd still just instantiate them directly here.  _Maybe_ there will be a day when we have enough options and a very flexible model, and it will make sense to allow this dependency to be injected again.  But for now, it's just added complexity that isn't necessary.

Basically, there was a problem when I masked out the scores per tag for the root node. The edge weights for the MST are just the max over the labels, but for actually choosing a label, we just take the argmax from over the labels for the given arc - so for the word which is the root of the tree (i.e has the root as it's parent), all of the label scores were zero - so it was arbitrarily picking the first one. I needed to separate the choosing of the labels to use the original unmasked scores, and only use the masked scores for decoding the arcs. Can I make that comment better?

The way we would usually handle this sort of multi-byte encoding is with a [`CodingStateMachine`](https://github.com/chardet/chardet/blob/master/chardet/codingstatemachine.py#L33) and [`CharDistributionAnalysis`](https://github.com/chardet/chardet/blob/master/chardet/chardistribution.py#L40)-based implementation. You can see the prober we have for UTF-8 (which is obviously single-byte) [here](https://github.com/chardet/chardet/blob/master/chardet/utf8prober.py), which only uses the `CodingStateMachien`, but the approach is close to what we would need to do for UTF-16 and UTF-32. In fact, through some crazy bit of coincidence, we already have [unused state machines for UTF-16LE and UTF-16BE in our code](https://github.com/chardet/chardet/blob/master/chardet/mbcssm.py#L390-L498) that I've never noticed until now. It appears to have been there since version 1.0, but never used.

Anyway, I know it would be a bit of work to switch this over to using `CodingStateMachine` like the UTF-8 prober, so I'm not going to require that to merge this, but if you don't do it, I'll probably end up switching this over to that eventually.

So my initial proposal was worded like this. It was in an email called "TLS Updates Proposal", let me know if you'd like me to send it again.

I think my main concerns were making Certbot useful to more people and to make it so those who disable our TLS updates for renewal (keeping in mind there are valid reasons to do so) don't suddenly have an update forced upon them through normal Certbot usage.

If a sysadmin wants to deploy a site with a TLS config they know shouldn't be modified, but they want to use a Certbot installer plugin for automated restarts on renewal, security enhancements like slowly increasing HSTS age and OCSP prefetching, etc. forcing them to take our TLS config first and revert it back seems unnecessary. This would make automating that sites deployment very hard without a deep understanding of the changes Certbot's going to make which I wouldn't expect them to have or consider stable and unchanging at this point. I also think adding/removing domains isn't an uncommon part of Certbot usage. If you have disabled TLS security updates and need to do this, do you have to manually revert pieces of your config in this case too?

In theory, there could be a separate flag for this, but it's hard for me to imagine when you'd want one without the other and it causes problems when people forget one of the flags.

Looks like the correct behavior for `fromBlock` is something like:
- `earliest`: from oldest block, i.e. all registered events since beggining of time
- `latest`: from most recent mined block
- `pending`: transactions not yet mined ( `> latest`)

nit: Making classes and structs `final` makes sure your class doesn't fall in one of the many inheritance traps (say not having virtual destructors) because someone inherited from it and you didn't anticipated it.
Also, making it `final` allows the compiler to better optimize some code like type casts.

In this particular case, it doesn't apply because the destructor of `OptimizePass` is already virtual, but it's still a good general C++ rule, and the compiler optimization point still applies.
